title: Reporting Call Prep
description:
This task must be fully updated by EOD Monday before the reporting call.

This document serves as:
  • Internal alignment between AM, CM, and SEO
  • Source of truth for call structure
  • Risk detection tool
  • Talking point preparation
No one should walk into the reporting call unprepared.

This prep aligns directly with the live call agenda.

AM should use this document to guide the call in real time.

No one should enter the call without this fully filled out.

SECTION 1: OPENING FRAME (AM)


What tone are we setting?

Client temperature this month:
 ☐ Strong
 ☐ Neutral
 ☐ Slightly concerned
 ☐ Frustrated

Notes from weekly pulse:


Any relationship items to acknowledge at opening?

Opening focus reminder:
What do we want the client to feel by minute 3?

SECTION 2: PERFORMANCE OVERVIEW (CM SECTION)


@LeadSEO please provide by Wednesday 12 PM:
  1. How is performance this month?
  2. Campaign health status: Green / Yellow / Red
  3. Areas of concern
  4. Anything stand out? Wins or things we need to be aware of?
  5. Do you need anything from the client?
  6. What is our main focus next month?
AM must review and confirm alignment with CM before call.

Risk Flag:
 ☐ No performance concerns
 ☐ Minor fluctuation
 ☐ Underperformance risk

If risk exists:
  • Has CM been aligned?
  • Has AM Lead been notified?
No surprises allowed on call.

SECTION 3: PERFORMANCE Q&A PREP


List anticipated performance questions:




Confirm:

☐ CM prepared for these
☐ Messaging aligned
☐ Time boundary plan set

SECTION 4: WORK COMPLETED (AM SECTION)


This mirrors the “Campaign Execution” portion of the call.

List in client-friendly language:
  • SEO deliverables completed:

  • Content published:

  • Links built:

  • Technical fixes:

  • Dev completed:

  • Wins (Sentinel, visibility gains, authority growth):

No internal jargon.

SECTION 5: WORK IN PROGRESS (AM)


  • Active SEO initiatives:

  • Content pipeline:

  • Technical initiatives:

  • Dev in progress:

  • Strategic pushes:

Must clearly show momentum.

SECTION 6: TEAM NEEDS (AM)


Do we need anything from the client?
  • Approvals:

  • Content:

  • Access:

  • Decisions:


If nothing needed, state clearly: None at this time.

SECTION 7: MONTH AHEAD VISION (AM)


This section drives confidence.

Answer clearly:
  • What is our main focus next month?
  • What are we monitoring closely?
  • What would success look like by next reporting call?

SECTION 8: FINAL Q&A PREP (AM)


Non-performance questions the client may raise:


Plan response or follow-up pathway.

Confirm:

☐ If unsure, defer and follow up
☐ CM will not re-enter unless pre-discussed

SECTION 9: ESCALATION CHECK


Before call, confirm:

☐ SEO updates received
☐ Dev updates confirmed
☐ Any blockers escalated
☐ AM Lead notified if risk exists
☐ CM aligned on any sensitive topics

If any box cannot be checked, escalate before call.

SECTION 10: CALL CONTROL REMINDERS (AM)


  • Set agenda at beginning
  • Protect time at minute 20
  • Transition clearly
  • Do not guess
  • Close confidently

First up are the location pages, they have several /areas-we-serve/ pillar pages that also split into location + service pages. Here's how I would like to tackle these:

https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1wfTv0vHTa7ABXjEcoECY86FYaLZxsETaDMCDZotkEro/edit?usp=sharing

There's also data behind this, looking at GSC data for the past 3 months, the pages getting the most clicks and impressions are the main city pages and not the city + service pages, so they're diluting SEO VALUE:

Once we have updated location page URLs, we need to update the https://acplushvac.com/areas-we-serve/ page, which does not have links to the actual service areas as we see on the homepage, causing a bad user experience and overall poor internal linking best practices. Need a setup similar to what we have on the homepage: (NB, we would have culled all the cooling and emergency links to just have the main location pages)

title: Include Sitemap In footer of Client websites
description:
Hi, this is inline w/ a group of tasks we have from a sheet. Here is the link for reference: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1qPfUUC1J2r1s7Ot8Y1PmIcje6WpyMszDhxHGz4TxDTk/edit?gid=0#gid=0

SEO added more info column D. 

I have included the task info here:
--- 
Hey Team!

To enhance our technical SEO baseline, improve crawl efficiency, and ensure consistent signaling across all client properties, It would be great to come to an agreement to outlining a new, standardized protocol for crawl management.

My suggestion is as follows:

Implement the following three directives on all client websites as part of our standard build and maintenance process.

1. XML Sitemap in robots.txt (Primary Directive)

This is the most effective way to inform search engines of all indexable URLs.

Action: Ensure the robots.txt file for every client domain contains a Sitemap directive pointing to the full URL of the XML sitemap.

Implementation Example:
User-agent: 
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap.xml

The "Why": This provides a clear, direct path for Google to find and crawl our most important pages, ensuring faster discovery and indexing of new content.

2. User-Facing HTML Sitemap (Footer)

This is for user experience and secondary crawl pathing.

Action: All client websites must have a user-facing HTML sitemap.

Implementation: This sitemap must be linked in the footer of every page on the site.

The "Why": This provides a clear navigation path for users, improves site accessibility, and   acts as a secondary crawl path for search engines to discover deep pages.

Strategic Crawl & Indexation Control

This is critical. We must be precise in telling crawlers what to crawl and what to index.

A. To Control Crawling (Using robots.txt)

We must Disallow crawlers from accessing low-value, non-SEO pages to conserve crawl budget.

Action: Audit and update the robots.txt file to Disallow non-public or utility sections of the site.
Implementation Examples:
Disallow: /wp-admin/

The WP sitemap page  plugin in perfect to use as a plugin for now

Regarding providing a robot.txt:

They differ per client especially regarding disallows but here is a comprehensive one that can be edited according to client needs especially regarding disallows:

User-agent: *

# --- Core WordPress File Disallows ---
# Block all core administrative and include folders.
Disallow: /wp-admin/
Disallow: /wp-includes/

# --- Crawl Budget Optimization ---
# Block non-media plugin and theme files from being crawled.
Disallow: /wp-content/plugins/
Disallow: /wp-content/themes/

# Block internal search result pages, which are low-quality, duplicate content.
Disallow: /?s=
Disallow: /search/

# Block other non-public WordPress files and pages.
Disallow: /wp-login.php
Disallow: /readme.html
Disallow: /trackback/
Disallow: /xmlrpc.php

# --- Allow Directives for Critical Files ---
# Explicitly ALLOW critical AJAX and asset files that Google
# may need for rendering, even if their parent folder is disallowed.
Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php
Allow: /wp-content/uploads/

# --- Sitemap Directive ---
# This is the primary directive to tell all search engines where our XML sitemap is located.
# [IMPORTANT: Replace with the client's actual sitemap URL]
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap_index.xml

Also, just an example of placement. Sitemap listed at the very bottom of the footer on the left followed by privacy policy etc.

* In the footer we will use the page /sitemap/ and not the XML sitemap.  The XML sitemap is for Google & the robots.txt.
